title: Shortcrust pastry, blind-baked
subtitle: A 23 cm tart case, ready for any filling
excerpt: Plain shortcrust at half-fat-to-flour, rubbed in cold, brought together with the smallest amount of water. A 30-minute chill, a 20-minute blind bake, a 5-minute uncover. The case the recipe takes its time on so the filling doesn't have to.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. "Short Crust for Sweet Pastry" sets the half-fat-to-flour proportion still used today. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, documents the cold-water minimum and the rest-before-roll discipline. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), gives the blind-baking sequence with weights on the base.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap the unbaked disc tightly and freeze for up to 3 months. Defrost in the fridge overnight before rolling. The blind-baked case freezes for up to 1 month, double-wrapped.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly to two cases — roll and chill both before baking.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the disc the day before; chill overnight in clingfilm. Blind-bake the case the morning of serving.

recipeTools.notes: For lining the case before blind-baking.
Ceramic or metal. Dried rice or chickpeas work too.

glossaryTerms.definition: Working cold fat into flour with the fingertips until the mixture looks like fine breadcrumbs. Each speck of fat stays separate; the result is a short, tender crumb.
Baking an unfilled pastry case so the crust sets before any wet filling goes in. The case is lined with paper and weighted with beans so the base stays flat.

content.text: A 23 cm shortcrust case, blind-baked and ready for any filling — quiche, treacle tart, custard tart, jam tart. The proportion is the British baseline: half fat to flour, the smallest amount of cold water needed to bring the dough together, a 30-minute chill before rolling. Forty-five minutes of active and resting work; twenty-five minutes in the oven.

Cold matters. Cold butter; cold water; a cold worktop if you can manage it. Warm fat melts into the flour and produces a tough crust instead of a short one. If your kitchen runs warm, chill the bowl and the flour in the fridge for ten minutes before you start.

attrs.title: Why blind-bake
attrs.body: Wet fillings (egg custard, treacle, fruit syrup) saturate raw pastry before the heat sets the crust, and you end up with a soggy base. Baking the case empty first lets the crust dry and crisp; the filling goes into a stable shell. The bean weights stop the base puffing up while it sets.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: ice-cold; you may not need all of it

content.text: For a sweet case (treacle tart, jam tart, fruit tart), add a tablespoon of caster sugar to the flour at the start. For a savoury case (quiche, cheese-and-onion tart), leave it plain.
Method
Rub the butter into the flour, 5 minutes
Weigh {{plain-flour}} of plain flour into a mixing bowl with {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine sea salt. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of cold cubed unsalted butter. Lift the cubes through your fingertips and
rub them into the flour

with a light flicking motion: fingertips only, not palms. After three or four minutes the mixture looks like coarse breadcrumbs with no butter pieces larger than a small pea. If your hands warm the butter, tip the bowl into the fridge for five minutes and come back to it.
Bring the dough together with the smallest amount of water

Sprinkle {{water}} of ice-cold water over the crumbs, a spoonful at a time, cutting through with a palette knife or your fingertips. Stop adding water the moment the crumbs start clumping together; you may not need all three tablespoons. Press the dough into a single piece — don't knead it. Working it past the bringing-together point develops gluten, and gluten makes a tough crust.
Chill, 30 minutes

Flatten the dough into a 2 cm thick disc, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. The rest does two things: the water finishes hydrating the flour evenly, and the gluten that did form relaxes so the case won't shrink in the oven. Skip the chill and the case shrinks down the sides of the tin as it bakes.
Roll, line, and chill again

Flour the worktop lightly. Set the chilled disc in the centre and roll outwards in short strokes, turning the disc a quarter turn every two or three strokes. Aim for a circle 3–4 mm thick and about 4 cm wider than the tin. Roll the pastry loosely around the rolling pin, lift it over the tin, and unroll it across the surface. Press the pastry gently into the corners and up the fluted sides; don't stretch it. Trim the overhang with a knife or roll the pin across the top of the tin. Prick the base eight or ten times with a fork. Rest the lined tin in the fridge for another 15 minutes while the oven heats.
Blind-bake, 20 minutes
Heat the oven to 190°C fan.
Blind-bake

by tearing a square of baking paper larger than the tin, crumpling it into a ball, smoothing it out, and pressing it into the chilled case. Fill with baking beans (or dried rice or dried chickpeas) right to the top; the weight needs to support the sides as well as the base. Slide the tin onto the middle shelf and bake for 20 minutes.
Uncover and finish, 5–10 minutes

Lift the paper and beans out carefully — they are very hot. The base should look matte and pale-blonde, not raw. Slide the case back into the oven uncovered for 5 to 10 minutes; the base finishes drying and turns a soft gold. Watch the edges: pull the case at the first sign of deep colour. Cool the case on a rack while you make the filling.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: If something went sideways
attrs.intro: The five ways a shortcrust case goes wrong, and what to fix next time.

items.cause: Worked too much after the water went in.
items.fix: Stop the moment the crumbs clump. Use a palette knife, not your hands, to cut the water through.

items.cause: Pastry stretched into the tin, or skipped chill before baking.
items.fix: Press gently into the corners — never pull. The 15-minute rest after lining is non-negotiable.

items.cause: Filling went in before the case was fully dried.
items.fix: Bake covered for 20 minutes, then uncovered for at least 5 — the base should look matte, not glossy.

items.cause: Pastry too thin, OR pricked too aggressively.
items.fix: Roll to 3–4 mm; prick eight to ten times, no more. The fork holes are for steam, not aeration.

items.cause: Butter too warm when rubbed in.
items.fix: Cube the butter, return to the fridge for 10 minutes before rubbing in. Cold fingers help — run them under cold water and dry before starting.

content.text: What fills this case

Treacle tart takes 400 g golden syrup, 150 g fresh white breadcrumbs, the zest of a lemon, baked at 180°C fan for 25 minutes. Quiche Lorraine takes 200 ml double cream, 3 eggs, 150 g cooked smoked bacon, 50 g grated Gruyère, baked at 160°C fan for 30 minutes. Custard tart takes 300 ml whole milk, 200 ml double cream, 4 yolks, 50 g caster sugar, a grating of nutmeg, baked at 150°C fan for 35 minutes. The case is the foundation; the fillings are the recipes that get to vary.
Where this pastry lives

Half-fat-to-flour shortcrust is the foundation of British pie- and tart-making. Beeton's 1861 chapter on pastry lists the same proportion as the household standard; Acton's earlier recipes use it for both sweet and savoury cases. The blind-baking sequence with weighted paper is the 20th-century refinement Florence White documents in 1932. The recipe here is the household case unchanged in its proportions for nearly two centuries.
